title: Fitting a waste pipe air admittance valve
subtitle: Install an AAV on a 40 mm or 50 mm waste pipe to allow venting without extending the pipe through the roof
excerpt: How to fit an air admittance valve on a basin, bath, or kitchen sink waste pipe. Covers cutting into the waste run or connecting to the stack, fitting the AAV in the correct orientation, and checking the valve opens and closes correctly.
sourceNotes: UK plumbing trade practice. BS EN 12380 covers air admittance valves for drainage systems. Water Regulations Advisory Scheme (WRAS) guidance on AAV positioning. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A one-way valve fitted to the top of a waste or soil pipe branch to admit air into the system when water runs through the waste, preventing trap siphonage. A negative pressure wave caused by water falling through the stack sucks air in through the AAV; when the flow stops, the valve closes and the seal prevents sewer gas escaping. AAVs can be used as an alternative to extending a soil pipe through the roof, subject to BS EN 12380 positioning requirements (they must be accessible, in a ventilated space, and above the highest trap on the connected branch. Not permitted on the main soil stack above a WC unless a separate external vent is also present.

content.text: prevents trap siphonage on a basin or sink waste run when extending the vent pipe to the roof is not practical. The approach is fitting the AAV to a branch tee or swept tee on the waste pipe in the correct position) above the highest trap and in an accessible, ventilated space.

The AAV must sit above the spill-over level of the highest appliance connected to the branch (above the rim of the highest basin if fitting on a branch. It must be in a ventilated void such as a boxing, an under-sink cupboard with an open grille, or a wall cavity with a ventilated access. Do not fit in a sealed unventilated void.

Cut a section from the waste pipe and insert a 90-degree swept tee) either solvent-weld for rigid pipe or a push-fit tee for plastic solvent-weld runs already in the wall. Turn the branch socket upward (the AAV connects vertically. On some basin waste installations, the trap itself includes an integral AAV socket at the top of the trap body; use this if present.

Screw or push the AAV onto the branch connection, following the thread or push-fit engagement on the specific valve. The AAV must be vertical) the internal rubber or EPDM disc closes under gravity. Apply a half turn of PTFE tape on any threaded connection before screwing the valve on. Hand-tight plus a half turn is sufficient (do not overtighten.

Run water through the appliance. You should hear air being admitted through the AAV as the flow draws a negative pressure) a faint hiss from the valve. The trap seal should remain intact after the water drains; a gurgling trap after draining indicates the AAV is not opening fast enough, usually because it is too far below the trap level.
